# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, comparative performance results for coding agents with and without repository context files, behavioral trace findings about tool use and reasoning, and explicit limitations about scope and generalizability.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
Across multiple coding agents and LLMs, context files tended to reduce task success rates versus providing no repository context.

## Source Excerpt

"Across multiple coding agents and LLMs, we find that context files tend to reduce task success rates compared to providing no repository context"

## Note

This is the paper’s top-level aggregate finding and does not by itself separate LLM-generated from developer-provided files.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Context files increased inference cost by over 20% in the reported evaluations.

## Source Excerpt

"while also increasing inference cost by over 20%"

## Note

The abstract states an aggregate increase and does not attach this number to a single dataset or model.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., Introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
On average, developer-provided context files improved performance by 4% relative to omitting context files.

## Source Excerpt

"developer-provided files only marginally improve performance compared to omitting them entirely (an increase of 4% on average)"

## Note

The source frames this as a marginal improvement rather than a large gain.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., Introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
On average, LLM-generated context files decreased agent performance by 3% relative to no context files.

## Source Excerpt

"while LLM-generated context files have a small negative effect on agent performance (a decrease of 3% on average)"

## Note

The source describes the negative effect as small.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 3 / AGENTbench overview
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
AGENTbench contains 138 instances from 12 repositories that all include developer-written context files.

## Source Excerpt

"The benchmark contains 138 138 unique instances, covering both bug-fixing and feature addition tasks across 12 12 recent and niche repositories, which all feature developer-written context files."

## Note

This evidence describes the benchmark composition rather than a performance outcome.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 3.2 Environment Set-Up
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The authors kept only pull requests whose setup produced a machine-readable test-results dictionary with at least one passing test, which retained 87% of filtered instances.

## Source Excerpt

"We only keep PRs where the resulting dictionary contains at least one passing test, which corresponds to 87 % 87\\% of the filtered instances."

## Note

This is a benchmark-construction filter, not a result about context-file usefulness.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 3.2 Generating Unit Tests / Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The generated tests in AGENTbench achieved 75% average coverage of modified code.

## Source Excerpt

"The resulting tests achieve an average coverage of 75% of the modified code (see Table ˜ 1 )."

## Note

Table 1 also reports a minimum of 2.5% and maximum of 100% coverage, but this unit keeps only the average.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
LLM-generated context files reduced average resolution rate by 0.5% on SWE-bench Lite and 2% on AGENTbench.

## Source Excerpt

"In more detail, the average resolution rate is reduced by 0.5 % 0.5\\% and 2 % 2\\% on average on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench , respectively."

## Note

The effect is small in absolute size and differs by dataset, so this result should stay attached to the dataset split.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2 / Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
LLM-generated context files increased average steps by 2.45 on SWE-bench Lite and 3.92 on AGENTbench, with average cost increases of 20% and 23% respectively.

## Source Excerpt

"the context files increase the # steps in every setting on average by 2.45 2.45 and 3.92 3.92 steps, respectively, which leads to a cost increase of 20% and 23% on average, respectively"

## Note

This unit combines the paper’s dataset-level averages for steps and cost under LLM-generated context files.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Developer-provided context files outperformed LLM-generated context files for all four evaluated agents.

## Source Excerpt

"We observe that the developer-provided context files outperform the LLM-generated ones for all four agents"

## Note

The source separately notes that developer-provided files still increased steps and cost, so this relative win does not imply an overall efficiency improvement.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Developer-provided context files increased average step count by 3.34 and increased cost by as much as 19%.

## Source Excerpt

"However, developer-provided context files also increase the average number of steps and costs required to solve the task, on average by 3.34 3.34 steps and at most 19%, respectively."

## Note

This is a cost/efficiency penalty attached to the human-written context-file setting.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2 Context files do not provide effective overviews
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Among the 12 developer-provided context files in AGENTbench, 8 included a dedicated codebase overview and 4 explicitly enumerated directories and subdirectories.

## Source Excerpt

"Across the 12 developer-provided context files in AGENTbench , 8 include a dedicated codebase overview, with 4 explicitly enumerating and describing the directories and subdirectories in the repository."

## Note

This describes prevalence of overview sections, not whether they improved task performance.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2 Context files do not provide effective overviews
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Context files did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before agents first interacted with a file modified in the original PR patch.

## Source Excerpt

"Both on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench the presence of context files does not meaningfully reduce this metric"

## Note

The metric excludes the 3% of instances where the agent never interacted with any file modified in the original patch.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: incident
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2 Context files do not provide effective overviews
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In manual trace inspection, GPT-5.1 mini often spent extra steps locating and rereading context files even though those files were already included in the agent context.

## Source Excerpt

"we observe in manual trace inspection that this increase is due to it (i) issuing multiple commands to find the context files and (ii) reading them (multiple times) despite them being already included in the agent’s context"

## Note

This is a model-specific observational explanation from manual trace inspection, not a controlled causal test across all agents.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.2 Context files are redundant documentation
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
When other documentation was removed, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation.

## Source Excerpt

"In this setting, where context files are the only source of documentation available, LLM-generated context files not only consistently improve performance by 2.7 2.7 % on average, but also outperform developer-written documentation."

## Note

The paper reports this under an altered evaluation condition where documentation files, example code, and docs/ were manually removed, and Claude Code was excluded due to cost.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: claim-support
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
When context files were present, agents ran more tests, searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.

## Source Excerpt

"We find that, across all models, when context files are present, the coding agents run more tests.
They also tend to navigate the repository more: they search more files ( grep ), read more files, and write more files.
Lastly, adding context files causes agents to use more repository-specific tooling"

## Note

This evidence directly describes observed behavioral changes in traces.
```

### file: evidence-17.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-17
kind: claim-support
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.3 Instructions in context files are typically followed
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Agents used uv about 1.6 times per instance when uv was mentioned in the context file, versus fewer than 0.01 times when it was not mentioned.

## Source Excerpt

"For instance, uv is used 1.6 times per instance on average when mentioned in the context files, compared to fewer than 0.01 times when it is not mentioned"

## Note

The source presents this as evidence that instructions in context files are generally followed.
```

### file: evidence-18.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-18
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.3 Following context files requires more thinking
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
LLM-generated context files increased average reasoning-token use by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite, and by 14% and 10% respectively on AGENTbench.

## Source Excerpt

"In Figure ˜ 7 , we show that LLM-generated context files indeed increase the average number of reasoning tokens by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite (respectively 14% and 10% on AGENTbench )"

## Note

The paper uses adaptive reasoning-token usage as evidence that added context-file instructions made tasks harder for these models.
---

"In Figure ˜ 7 , we show that LLM-generated context files indeed increase the average number of reasoning tokens by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite (respectively 14% and 10% on AGENTbench )"

## Note

The paper uses adaptive reasoning-token usage as evidence that added context-file instructions made tasks harder for these models.

```markdown
---

evidence_id: evidence-15

confidence: high

contradiction_relevance: tension
---
When other documentation was removed, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation.

"In this setting, where context files are the only source of documentation available, LLM-generated context files not only consistently improve performance by 2.7 2.7 % on average, but also outperform developer-written documentation."

```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: claim-support
citation: Gloaguen et al., Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
When context files were present, agents ran more tests, searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.

## Source Excerpt

"We find that, across all models, when context files are present, the coding agents run more tests.
They also tend to navigate the repository more: they search more files ( grep ), read more files, and write more files.
Lastly, adding context files causes agents to use more repository-specific tooling"

## Note

This evidence directly describes observed behavioral changes in traces.
```

LLM-generated context files increased average reasoning-token use by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite, and by 14% and 10% respectively on AGENTbench.

## Source Excerpt

"In Figure ˜ 7 , we show that LLM-generated context files indeed increase the average number of reasoning tokens by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite (respectively 14% and 10% on AGENTbench )"

## Note

The paper uses adaptive reasoning-token usage as evidence that added context-file instructions made tasks harder for these models.
